segments.s113-g9: This clause expresses an inability/impersonal negative: the verb form نیارست (here not matching the provided lexical senses) plus کردن forms a verbal phrase meaning 'could not be done / was not possible.' Word order is inverted (object/phrase before the subject کس), so literally 'was not possible to make anyone pass there.' آنجا (= 'there') marks the place and گذر (= 'passage/pass') names what could not be effected.
segments.s113-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/through'; the following nouns are a list of obstacles. Note دیوان here means supernatural demons (sense not listed in the lexical choices), and نر qualifies شیران as male lions. The whole segment gives the reason/route that made passage impossible.
segments.s113-g11: همان نیز 'that one likewise' (subject phrase). کامد is an archaic/perfective verb meaning 'came/appeared'; نیابد is the negative form of یابیدن 'to find' (here 'did not find' / 'could not obtain'); رها functions as the thing sought ('release' / 'freedom'). The clause thus links a coming action with the inability to attain release.
segments.s113-g12: ز + چنگ = 'from the claw/grip'; بداندیش is an adjective 'ill‑minded, malicious' modifying the following noun; نر specifies 'male' and اژدها is 'dragon.' The whole phrase is an ablative/agentive phrase indicating from whom/what release was impossible.
segments.s113-g13: سوی 'toward' marks direction. رخش here is the name/steed (Rakhsh) and رخشنده is an epithet 'radiant'; بنهاد is a past verb 'placed/put' (here idiomatically 'turned/settled') and روی is 'face.' The construction 'روی بنهاد' = 'set his face toward' (i.e., 'faced toward').
segments.s113-g14: دوان (a verbal adjective/participle meaning 'running/galloping') + اسپ 'steed' with شد as the perfective/auxiliary ('became' / 'began to'). سوی دیهیم جوی gives the destination: سوی 'toward', دیهیم 'rural/village' qualifies جوی 'stream'. Together: 'the steed began to gallop toward the village stream.'
segments.s113-g15: همی is a frequentative/adverb ('continually/kept on'); کوفت is a past tense verb 'struck/pounded'; بر خاک 'upon the earth'; رویینه 'armored/iron‑covered' modifies سم 'hoof' (here 'iron‑shod hoof'). The clause describes repeated stomping: 'he kept striking the armored earth with his hoof.'
segments.s113-g16: چو (= 'when/like') introduces a simile/temporal clause. تندر 'thunder' + خروشید 'roared' gives 'it roared like thunder'; و 'and' links the second verb افشاند 'scattered/shot forth' with object دم 'breath' (here 'blew out / snorted'). The clause depicts a thunderous roar and an explosive exhalation.
segments.s113-g97: که is the subordinating particle 'that/for' introducing this clause and links it to the previous speech; پیشم means 'before me / in my presence' (prepositional phrase). The repeated چه ... و چه ... و چه ... is a correlative listing 'whether ... or ... or ...' and there is no verb in this segment — the predicate comes in the next segment, so these nouns (شیر، دیو، پیل) are the alternatives being presented.
segments.s113-g98: This is the predicate/noun phrase: بیابان بی‌آب is a nominal phrase 'desert without water' (بی‌آب is a negative adjective formed with the prefix بی- and follows the noun in the poem), و links the two places, and دریای نیل means 'the sea of the Nile' or 'the Nile's sea' (poetic usage). The whole segment supplies the locations that 'lie before me'.
segments.s113-g99: بداندیش functions as a substantive/adjective meaning 'ill‑wisher(s)/malicious ones' and بسیار here means 'many' (many such people). و‌گر is the classical conditional 'but if/if on the other hand', and اندکیست is an older fused form meaning 'there are few/it is small in number' — the clause contrasts the frequent case with the rare one.
segments.s113-g100: چو here is the temporal 'when'; خشم 'anger' is the object of آورم, which is first‑person singular present 'I bring/call forth' (so 'when I bring forth anger' = 'when I become angry'). پیش چشمم is a locative phrase 'before my eyes' (پیش + چشم + possessive -م), and یکیست is a predicative 'it is one / they are as one' — poetically saying that, in the face of his wrath, even a few enemies count as one (i.e., are all the same/insignificant).
segments.s113-g17: This is a temporal subordinate clause: تهمتن (the proper name) + چُو (classical subordinating conjunction 'when') + از خواب ('from/out of sleep') + بیدار شد (past tense, compound sense 'became awake'). Word order follows older Persian: subordinate marker before the verb, verb at the end.
segments.s113-g18: Main clause with predicate شد at the end: سر پر خرد پر ز پیکار شد is a poetic stacking of two 'full-of' phrases. سر ('head') + پر ('full (of)') + خرد ('thought/wisdom') then another پر ('full (of)') + ز ('of/from', poetic) + پیکار ('battle/strife'); شد ('became') closes the clause. The repeated پر ... پر construction emphasizes two simultaneous qualities.
segments.s113-g19: Prepositional phrase + verb: به گرد بیابان ('around the plain/desert') + یکی ('once/a certain' — here a brief poetic device) + بنگرید (past/formal 3rd‑person perfect of نگرِستن 'to look'). The verb is final, as is typical; one full clause meaning 'he looked around the plain'.
segments.s113-g20: Independent clause with شد as auxiliary/copula: شد ('became' / past auxiliary) + آن ('that/the') + اژدهای (poetic genitive form of اژدها 'dragon') + دژم (adjective 'grim') + ناپدید (predicate adjective/participle 'vanished/gone'). Literal structure is 'became that grim dragon vanished' → 'that grim dragon had disappeared.'
segments.s113-g21: Poetic, slightly elliptical clause ending with کرد (the deictic/historical past 'did/made'). ابا appears here in an archaic/poetic use before the subject رخش (the horse); the core action is رخش ... پیکار کرد ('Rakhsh fought'). بر introduces the opponent ('against') and خیره is an adjective/nominal 'the staring/astonished one'. The final کرد completes the compound verbal idea 'performed combat'.
segments.s113-g22: ازان کاو is a classical/poetic formula meaning 'from that one whom…' (کاو here functions as an archaic relative/connector 'که او'), followed by سرخفته ('laid low/asleep') + بیدار ('awake') + کرد (past 'made/caused'). Together: 'from that one he caused the sleeper to wake' — i.e. he woke him. The main verb کرد appears at the clause end.
segments.s113-g23: Temporal clause introduced by دگر باره ('again, another time') + چون ('when') + شد (past 'became/went') + به خواب اندرون ('into sleep, inside'). The verb شد is the past marker and appears before the locative complement; overall: 'when (he) once more went into sleep inside'.
segments.s113-g24: Prepositional clause: ز تاریکی ('from the darkness') + آن اژدها ('that dragon') + شد برون (شد 'became' + برون 'out/forth') — a classical way to say 'that dragon came out of the darkness', with شد + برون forming the verbal idea of emerging.
segments.s113-g49: Three past intransitive verbs appear in sequence describing an implied subject (omitted, typical in Persian epic): خروشید (past of خروشیدن) 'it roared' then جوشید 'it gushed/erupted', linked by و as a simple conjunction, and برکند 'it uprooted / tore up' takes خاک as its direct object. Word order here places the verbs before the explicit object (خاک) because the subject is left implicit.
segments.s113-g50: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'by'; نعلش is نعل + possessive suffix 'his horseshoe'. زمین شد uses شد (past auxiliary/verb 'became') with زمین as subject meaning 'the ground became' and همه چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective phrase ('all split, gaping') intensifying the result. The phrase therefore indicates that, by/under his horseshoe, the ground became entirely rent.
segments.s113-g51: چو here means 'when' (temporal). The verb phrase بیدار شد (literally 'became awake') is in the past; poetic word order places the verb before the subject رستم. از خواب خوش is a prepositional phrase 'from/ out of pleasant sleep' modifying the waking.
segments.s113-g52: برآشفت is a past intransitive indicating a sudden agitation or rising ('sprang up / grew angry / was agitated'); با is the instrumental 'with'. باره here (sense chosen as 'load/burden') functions idiomatically with دستکش ('glove') to mean roughly 'with (his) glove in hand' or 'by the weight/hold of the glove' — an archaic compressed expression indicating he seized or had his glove when he sprang up.
segments.s113-g53: چنان 'thus/so' introduces the manner; ساخت is the past tense 'made' and, in poetic inversion, the agent جهان‌آفرین ('the World‑Creator') follows the verb. روشن is the result/state 'bright' (direct object/complement of ساخت). The verb + complement order and the postposed subject are typical of high-register/poetic Persian.
segments.s113-g54: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'. پنهان here is used as the verb 'to hide' and نکرد is the negated past ('did not do' = 'did not hide'). The object اژدها را ('the dragon', marked by را) appears before the verb, while زمین ('the earth') is the subject; the literal order reads 'the earth did not hide the dragon.'
segments.s113-g55: برآن is a locative/relational adverbial 'on/at that' referring back to the preceding 'darkness' (تیرگی). Poetic word order places the subject رستم after the adverbial and the object او را ('him', with را marking definiteness) immediately before the verb بدید ('saw', past). The clause thus reads 'At that darkness Rostam saw him.'
segments.s113-g56: سبک modifies تیغ (here 'a light [in weight] sword'); تیز is an attributive adjective 'sharp'. از میان is an adverbial/locative 'from the midst / out of the middle', and برکشید is a past transitive 'he drew up / hefted / unsheathed' (here 'drew his sword'). The subject is carried over from the previous clause (Rostam).
segments.s113-g25: به بالین رستم is a locative phrase ('to/at Rostam's bedside'); note the poetic juxtaposition of بالین and رستم without an explicit ezāfe. تگ آورد رخش is a compact verb phrase: تگ functions adverbially ('close, tight/near') and آورد is the past tense 'brought/placed' used here intransitively, with رخش (the steed) placed after the verb by poetic inversion — i.e., 'Rakhsh brought/placed (himself) near.'
segments.s113-g26: همی is the habitual/continuous marker ('kept/was continually'); کند is the present/simple verbal base 'does/makes' used in narrative to express repeated action; خاک here means 'dust/earth' (the object). The second half, همی کرد پخش, repeats the habitual marker with کرد ('made/did') plus پخش ('a scattering' / 'to scatter') — together 'kept scattering (dust)'.
segments.s113-g27: دگرباره 'again' precedes the verb phrase. بیدار شد is a compound where بیدار ('awake') is followed by شد ('became' / past auxiliary) to mean 'became awake' or 'awoke'. خفته مرد is a noun phrase 'the sleeping man' with the participle خفته placed before مرد in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s113-g28: برآشفت is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'was agitated / sprang up'. The coordinating و links this to رخسارگان کرد زرد, where رخسارگان (plural 'faces/cheeks/countenances') is the object and کرد ('made') is causative; زرد ('pale') is the result predicate — 'made his countenance pale.'
segments.s113-g29: بیابان ('the plain/desert') is the domain being surveyed. همه سر به سر is an idiomatic phrase 'all over / from end to end' (literally 'head to head'), and بنگرید is the classical 3rd-person narrative form of نگریدن 'to look' — here translated 'looked/looked about.'
segments.s113-g30: بجز introduces an exception 'except'. تیرگی شب is the noun phrase 'the darkness of night'. به دیده is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with (his) eye(s)' and ندید is the negated past of دیدن 'did not see' — together: 'he saw with his eyes nothing except the darkness of night.'
segments.s113-g31: بدان is a compressed address 'to that' (poetic phrasing: به + دان in the text), so بدان مهربان رخش = 'to that kind Rakhsh'. بیدار here functions as the quoted imperative 'be awake' and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' — i.e., 'he said to his kind steed, "Be awake."'
segments.s113-g32: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). تاریکی شب is 'the darkness of night' as subject; بخواهی is the subjunctive/optative sense of خواستن here ('will/would want; will try') and نهفت (adjectival/verb form 'to hide, to be hidden') completes the idea — 'for the darkness of night will seek to hide.' This is an archaic/poetic subjunctive construction.
segments.s113-g65: This clause states location and belonging. صداندرصد (literally “a hundred in a hundred”) is an emphatic adverb meaning “entirely/wholly.” از دشت is the prepositional phrase “on/from the plain” and جای منست is a poetic copular phrase: جای (place) + منست (an older/poetic adjectival/copying form) = “is my proper place” (i.e. ‘belongs to me’ or ‘befits me’). The order (adverb — prepositional phrase — nominal predicate) is typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s113-g66: This is another nominal predicate describing the sky above the plain. بلند modifies آسمانش (“its sky” with the possessive suffix -ش), and هوای منست is a verbless/adjectival construction meaning roughly “its air/breeze is mine” or “its climate suits me.” منست again functions as a poetic copula/adjective. The sequence adjective → noun → predicate is a compact poetic description.
segments.s113-g67: Grammatically this clause centers on an (elliptical) negative or modal verb followed by an infinitive: [نیارد] + گذشتن (the infinitive ‘to pass’) + the locative phrase به سر بر عقاب (‘over the head on an eagle’). نیارد is archaic/obscure here; in context it functions to block or deny the action expressed by the infinitive (i.e. ‘one cannot / it will not be allowed to pass’). The prepositional grouping به سر بر عقاب is literal ‘to/over the head upon an eagle.’
segments.s113-g68: This clause is negative-present in form: نبیند is the negative form of دیدن (to see), used here for third-person present/optative sense ‘does not see’ or ‘no ... sees.’ ستاره is subject ‘star,’ زمینش is ‘its land’ (زمین + possessive -ش), and به خواب is an adverbial phrase ‘asleep’ or ‘in sleep.’ Word order is subject → verb → object/adverbial, though Persian often permits flexible order in poetry.
segments.s113-g69: بدو is the dative clitic ‘to him’ (to/unto him). اژدها is the noun subject ‘the dragon,’ and گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (here used with inverted word order: dative → subject → verb). نام تو چیست is a direct question embedded after the reporting verb: نام (name) + تو (your) + چیست (what-is), i.e. ‘What is your name?’ The copula ست (in the form چیست) appears as the final element, standard in Persian questions.
segments.s113-g70: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or consequence. زاینده (literally ‘the begetter’ or ‘the one who gave life’) is the noun marked by the object marker را, so زاینده را is a definite object/topic. بر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘over/for you’ and باید is the modal ‘must/ought,’ taking the following verb گریست (an infinitive/old-form of ‘to weep/lament’). The literal sequence is ‘that the begetter must weep for you,’ i.e. ‘even your sire must lament you.’
segments.s113-g71: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact poetic phrase meaning ‘gave such an answer’ (چنین ‘thus/so’ + داد ‘gave’ + پاسخ ‘answer’). که introduces the content of the reply. من رستمم is the identification clause: من (I) + رستمم (Rostam with the final -م acting as the 1st-person copular suffix ‘I am’ in this idiom), so ‘I am Rostam.’ The clause uses classical/poetic copular morphology rather than modern separate ‘هستم.’
segments.s113-g72: This is a list of origins/sources introduced by the prepositions ز / از (poetic short z then از): ز دستان و از سام و از نیرمم = ‘from Dastān and from Sam and from my Niram/my strength.’ The repeated از/ز marks provenance or lineage; و is the coordinating conjunction. Several proper names/terms here are archaic or ambiguous in isolation (poetry often strings ancestors and qualities together), so the structure should be read as cumulative ‘of/from X and Y and Z.’
segments.s113-g1: ز دشت اندر آمد یکی اژدها is an inverted, somewhat archaic word order: ز دشت 'from the plain' (prepositional phrase), اندر آمد 'came in/arrived', and یکی اژدها 'a certain dragon' (یکی before the noun = 'a certain'). The verb آمد is final for that clause and мобes the action onto the subject 'یکی اژدها'.
segments.s113-g2: کزو پیل گفتی نیابد رها: کزو is a relative/locative 'from which / from whom' linking back to the dragon; پیل is the (implied) agent/affected creature 'elephant'. نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not / cannot find' (classical use), and رها is the predicate meaning 'freedom / release'. The clause means literally 'from which the elephant cannot find release.'
segments.s113-g3: بدان جایگه بودش آرامگاه: here بدان is the fused locative 'به + آن' ('in/at that'), (split in the data as ب + دان), جایگه 'place', and بودش is the past copula with enclitic 'sh' meaning 'it was (his)'; آرامگاه is the predicate 'resting-place/tomb'. The clause states that that place was his tomb/resting place.
segments.s113-g4: نکردی ز بیمش برو دیو راه is an idiomatic, compact clause: ن-کردی (negative past 'did not/didn't dare' used impersonally), ز بیمش 'because of/from his fear', and برو دیو راه expresses motion/route — 'the demon (دیو) [would] go that way (راه)'. Together: 'no demon, out of fear of him, went that way.' Word order places the negated verb before the reason phrase and the motion phrase.
segments.s113-g5: بیامد جهانجوی را خفته دید: بیامد 'he came/arrived' (perfect/past), then جهانجوی را with the object marker را shows 'the world-seeker' as the thing seen, خفته 'asleep' (predicate adjective placed before the verb), and دید 'saw' is the past verb concluding the clause. The sequence is 'he came; he saw the world-seeker asleep.'
segments.s113-g6: بر او یکی اسپ آشفته دید: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'before/at him' or 'near him', یکی اسپ 'a certain horse' (indefinite phrase with یکی before the noun), آشفته is a postpositive adjective 'disturbed/agitated', and دید is the final past verb 'saw'. The clause means 'he saw near him a certain agitated horse.'
segments.s113-g7: پر اندیشه شد تا چه آمد پدید: پر اندیشه شد is an idiom 'became full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)'), where اندیشه 'thought' is followed by شد 'became' (change-of-state auxiliary). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / to see', and چه آمد پدید is an interrogative-content clause 'what came forth / what would appear' with آمد (came) + پدید (appear) forming a periphrastic 'came to appear'.
segments.s113-g8: که یارد بدین جایگاه آرمید: که introduces a clause 'that/because', یارد 'companion/helper' (here 'companion'), and بدین جایگاه is the locative 'in/at this place' (given in the data as ب + دین but functioning as the fused prepositional phrase), جایگاه 'place', and آرمید is past 'rested/lay at rest'. The clause says the companion had rested in that place.
segments.s113-g73: This pre-verbal phrase is a dative/recipient phrase introduced by به (to). تنها یکی (literally “only one”) modifies کینه‌ور, so the phrase reads as “that one, the vindictive (one)”; لشکرم is the possessive form (‘my army’) attached to لشکر. The whole group functions as a fronted noun phrase indicating the thing/person being considered or set aside before the main verb.
segments.s113-g74: به رخش marks the recipient (‘to Rakhsh’), with دلاور postpositively qualifying رخش as an epithet (‘the valiant Rakhsh’). زمین is the direct object being entrusted (here ‘land/ground’ / ‘the land’). بسپرم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن (‘I entrust / I will entrust’); Persian often places objects and recipients before the verb in poetry.
segments.s113-g75: برآویخت is a past (3rd sg.) poetic form meaning ‘he sprang upon / grappled’. با او (‘with him’) indicates the partner/target of the action; به جنگ اژدها uses به to mean ‘into/toward’ (into battle) and names the contest (‘the battle with the dragon’). Word order (verb before subject/objects) reflects epic poetic style.
segments.s113-g76: نیامد is negative past ‘did not come’. به فرجام means ‘to an end / to a resolution’. هم here works as an intensifier (‘even / also’), and the remaining items (ز و رها) are compressed poetic elements: ز (poetic fromِ = ‘from/of’), و (‘and/then’), رها (‘release/free’). Together the clause expresses that the fight/affair did not reach a conclusion and was not set free/released from him — i.e. it did not end and was not loosened.
segments.s113-g77: چو here is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. زور تن اژدها is a genitive chain ‘the strength of the dragon’s body’ (agent/possessor comes after possessed in Persian epic inversion). دید is past ‘saw’; رخش (the steed’s name) follows the verb as subject — a common poetic word order.
segments.s113-g78: کزان سان is a fixed poetic locution: کزان = ‘from which / thence’, سان = ‘so / thus / in that manner’ — together ‘in that way’. برآویخت is the past verb ‘he sprang up / leapt upon’. با introduces the companion/agent (‘with’); تاجبخش here is used as a proper name (postpositive companion).
segments.s113-g79: بمالید is the past tense ‘he rubbed’ (stem مالیدن). گوش is the object ‘ear’. اندر آمد شگفت is an idiomatic sequence: اندر آمد = ‘came into (him)’, شگفت = ‘wonder/astonishment’ — i.e. ‘astonishment came upon him’ (he was amazed). The clause sequence reflects narrative, with small clauses joined without conjunctions.
segments.s113-g80: بلند qualifies اژدها (‘the tall/lofty dragon’). را is the definite object marker. به دندان is an instrumental phrase ‘with/by (his) tooth/teeth’ (به used adverbially/ instrumental), and گرفت is the past verb ‘he seized / grabbed’. Together: ‘he seized the tall dragon with his teeth.’
segments.s113-g81: بدرید is a past-tense perfective verb (3rd sg.) meaning “tore” placed at the start for poetic force. کتفش is the direct object “its/ his shoulder,” with the possessive enclitic -ش; بدندان is an instrumental/ adverbial phrase “with the teeth” (literal “by-tooth”) placed after the object. چو شیر is a simile (“like a lion”) modifying the action.
segments.s113-g82: برو here functions as a brief narrative connector/particle (literary), introducing the immediate result. خیره شد is a two-word predicate: خیره (staring/astonished) + شد (became) = “became astonished/stood transfixed.” پهلوان دلیر is a noun phrase (champion + postpositive adjective) meaning “the brave hero.”
segments.s113-g83: بزد is the past-tense verb “he struck/hit.” تیغ is the instrument/object “sword.” و links the two verbs. بنداخت is another perfective verb “cast/ hurled” and is followed by the prepositional phrase از بر سرش — a compound sense ‘from off his head’ (از = from; بر used in the sense ‘off/upon’; سرش = his head with enclitic).
segments.s113-g84: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal idea: فرو (down/ downward, an intensifier) + ریخت (past “fell/poured”) = “poured down/ran down.” چون رود خون is a simile “like a river of blood.” از برش is a prepositional phrase “from him/it” (here referring back to the wound); the token برش in the list appears to be a fused form (بر + ش).
segments.s113-g85: زمین شد ناپدید is a nominal subject + verb + predicate: زمین (the ground) + شد (became) + ناپدید (vanished) = “the ground became vanished.” به زیر تنش is the locative phrase “under his body” (به زیر = beneath; تنش = his body). Word order is typical of epic verse, allowing the predicate and locative phrase to follow the subject.
segments.s113-g86: یکی چشمه خون is a noun phrase “a spring (one spring) of blood” (یکی = a certain/one; چشمه = spring; خون = blood) serving as the next clause’s subject. از برش is the source phrase “from him/from his side” (از = from; برش here reads as a fused form meaning ‘on him/from him’). بردمید is an archaic past verb meaning something like “rose up/ gushed forth” in this context (poetic usage).
segments.s113-g87: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” رستم is the subject (Rostam). برآن is a demonstrative/locator meaning “at/on/against that” (here “at that [dragon]”), and اژدهای دژم is the noun phrase “that grim/ sullen dragon” (اژدهای = dragon; دژم = grim/sullen) which the clause locates.
segments.s113-g88: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did/made) = “he looked” or “fixed his gaze.” برزد is an archaic intransitive verb here meaning “struck / came (at him).” یکی تیز دم is the agent/subject of that verb — “one sharp tail” — with یکی (one/a certain) and تیز (sharp) as premodifiers and دم (here used for “tail”/tip) as the noun. Note the poetic inversion: the tail phrase follows the verb.
segments.s113-g89: Literal order: noun + quantifier + preposition + pronoun + copula + adverb. «بیابان همه» (noun + «همه») means “the whole plain/desert”; «زیر او بود» is a simple past copular clause “lay beneath him.” «پاک» appears after the verb as a poetic/post‑copular adverb meaning “completely/utterly,” intensifying the statement.
segments.s113-g90: This is a sequence of modifiers and a noun: «روان خون گرم» = “flowing (روان) warm (گرم) blood (خون).” The prepositional cluster «از بر» is an archaic/poetic pairing meaning roughly “from upon / out of (the surface of).” «تیره خاک» = “dark earth/soil.” The phrase as a whole describes blood streaming from/over the dark ground.
segments.s113-g91: Subject («تهمتن») + agent/target expressed with «ازو» (“at him/of him”) + locative «در شگفتی» (“in astonishment”) + verb «بماند» at the end. Here «بماند» is a past narrative/resultative meaning “remained/was left” (he stayed in a state of astonishment).
segments.s113-g92: «همی» signals a continuing or habitual action (here: he kept doing this). «پهلوی نام یزدان بخواند» literally places the object phrase before the verb: “(to) the side / at the side (پهلوی) the name (نام) of God (یزدان) he called (بخواند).” «بخواند» in this context means “called/invoked” (habitual/past narrative).
segments.s113-g93: A standard classical Persian motion-and-action clause: «به آب اندر آمد» uses doubled directional markers («به» + «اندر») meaning “entered into the water.” Objects «سر و تن» (“head and body”) precede the verb «بشست» (past tense “he washed”), with the verbal prefix «بـ-» marking past/perfective action.
segments.s113-g94: Pattern «جهان جز به X نجست» = “in the world, except by X, (no one) ...” Here «جز» is the exception marker “except,” «به زور» = “by the force/might of,” and «جهانبان» is a title/name (‘world‑guardian’). The verb «نجست» is the past of جستن and here functions as “strove/attempted / sought to contend” — i.e., no one in the world attempted/opposed him except by the strength of the world‑guardian.
segments.s113-g95: «به یزدان» = “to God.” «چنین گفت» = “thus said”; in narrative this places manner («چنین») before «گفت» (he said). «کای» is a vocative particle (“O!”) addressing the addressee, and «دادگر» is a vocative/title meaning “the Just One / just ruler.”
segments.s113-g96: Subject «تو» + past verb «دادی» (“you gave”) + object «مرا» split here orthographically into «م» (personal pronoun stem) + «را» (definite object marker). The list order shows that split. The series «دانش و زور و فر» are the three direct objects joined by «و»: “knowledge, strength, and farr/royal glory.”
segments.s113-g33: سرم را همی باز داری ز خواب — This clause uses the object marker را after سرم ("my head" = a metonym for "my sleep"). همی is the archaic/prosodic present marker meaning an ongoing or habitual action ("you keep/still do"). باز دارد(ی) here is a verbal compound meaning "to keep back / prevent," so the literal order is object–(adv)–verb: “you (همی) keep (دارى) my head (سرم را) from sleep (ز خواب).”
segments.s113-g34: به بیداری من گرفتت شتاب — به + noun here gives the circumstance/occasion ("at/by my awakening"). گرفتت is perfective/simple past form with the clitic ـت ("took you / seized you"); idiomatically "شتاب گرفتت" = "haste took you / you were seized by haste." The phrase order places the circumstance first, then the verbal predicate with object clitic.
segments.s113-g35: گر این‌بار سازی چنین رستخیز — گر is the conditional conjunction "if." این‌بار "this time." سازی is the deverbal/abstract noun here meaning "making/setting up" ("to make/raise" something); چنین modifies the following noun, so "چنین رستخیز" = "such an uprising/commotion." Word order is typical poetic: condition-marker + time phrase + action-noun + modifier.
segments.s113-g36: سرت را ببرم به شمشیر تیز — سرت را is the definite object "your head." ببرم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive/optative form (used in threats as a future/performative: "I will cut off"); here it expresses the speaker's asserted action. به + instrument (به شمشیر) means "by/with a sword," and تیز is an adjective post‑modifying شمشیر ("a sharp sword").
segments.s113-g37: پیاده شوم سوی مازندران — پیاده شوم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/periphrastic phrase meaning "I will be on foot / I will dismount / I go on foot" (poetic future implication). سوی + proper name gives the direction "toward Mazandaran." Word order: circumstance (on foot) + verb + direction.
segments.s113-g38: کشم ببر و شمشمیر و گرز گران — کشم is a 1st‑person form meaning "I will take/draw/carry" (the verb کشیدن in a hortative/future sense here). The following list names the things taken: ببر ("tiger" — likely a tiger‑skin or the animal as a trophy), شمشمیر ("sword"), و گرز گران ("and a heavy mace"); گران post‑modifies گرز as an adjective ("heavy/weighty"). The verb comes before the list of objects in this poetic order.
segments.s113-g39: سیم ره به خواب اندر آمد سرش — اندر آمد is an archaic/poetic perfective construction meaning "entered into / fell into." Here به خواب اندر آمد = "fell asleep / entered sleep." The sequence places the occasion first (به خواب اندر آمد) and then the possessive noun سرش ("his head") which links to the next clause; note the older word order and the enclitic ش = "his."
segments.s113-g40: ز ببر بیان داشت پوشش برش — ز indicates source or relation ("from / because of / out of"). بیان داشت is a light‑verb collocation where بیان ("statement/expression") + داشت (here functioning as a causative/auxiliary) yields "expressed / revealed / showed." پوشش برش groups the noun پوشش ("covering / cloak") with برش, an attached element referencing the person/thing (here effectively "his covering" or "a covering upon him"). The whole clause describes that, from/with the tiger, a covering was shown/placed on his head.
segments.s113-g57: بغرید is a poetic past (3rd‑person plural) ‘they roared’ with the final ‑ند omitted (common in classical Persian). برسان is here a verb meaning ‘brought/delivered’ (again with the usual poetic contraction of final ‑د), so the clause reads roughly ‘the spring clouds roared and brought (it)’. ابر بهار (‘spring cloud[s]’) is the agent placed after the verbs for poetic order/emphasis.
segments.s113-g58: زمین is the subject ‘the earth’. کرد is the light verb ‘made/did’ used with the adjective پر to form ‘made full’ (زمین کرد پر آتش = ‘the earth was made full of fire’). پر آتش is ‘full of fire’; از کارزار gives the source/cause ‘from/because of the battle’. Word order (noun + کرد + predicate) is typical in Ferdowsi’s verse.
segments.s113-g59: بدان here is a fused adverbial/discourse particle (be‑ + دان) meaning ‘thereupon’ or ‘to that’ and links what follows to the prior event. اژدها گفت is ‘the dragon said’ (گفت is past ‘said’, placed after the subject as in Persian). بر گوی نام literally orders ‘say the name’: بر + گوی is an emphatic/poetic verbal phrase (‘utter/say’), and نام is the direct object.
segments.s113-g60: کزین پس = ‘from this (time) on / henceforth’ (کزین = ‘from this’, پس = ‘after/then’). تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun ‘you’. گیتی is ‘the world’. نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + بینی) used here as a future/impersonal ‘you shall not see’. به کام = ‘to (your) desire / to satisfaction’ (به + کام).
segments.s113-g61: نباید is an impersonal modal ‘it must not / it should not’. که introduces the subordinate clause (‘that’). بی‌نام = ‘without name / nameless’. بر دست من = ‘into/on my hand’ (بر + دست + من), i.e. ‘come into my power’. The clause expresses a prohibition: ‘you must not come into my hand nameless.’
segments.s113-g62: روانت is روان + enclitic ت = ‘your soul/spirit’. برآید is a present/subjunctive or future sense of برآمدن ‘to arise/come forth’ (here ‘will be taken away / shall rise’). ز = ‘from’, تاریک = ‘dark/obscure’, تن = ‘body’. Together: ‘your soul shall come forth from the dark body’ (i.e. your spirit will depart your dark body).
segments.s113-g63: چنین گفت = ‘thus said’ (چنین ‘thus/so’, گفت ‘said’). دژخیم is an epithet ‘cruel/oppressive’; نر specifies ‘male’ and together with اژدها makes ‘the male dragon’. Adjectives and epithets are placed before the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s113-g64: که introduces the content clause ‘that…’. از چنگ من = ‘from my claw/clutch’ (از = from, چنگ = talon/claw, من = my). کس نیابد رها = literal ‘no one finds release’: کس ‘anyone/no one’ with نیابد (ن + یابد) ‘does not find / will not attain’, and رها is the noun ‘release/freeing’. The negative is carried by نیابد, so the clause means no one can escape the dragon’s grasp.
segments.s113-g41: بغرید is a past 3rd‑person singular verb meaning “roared/bellowed.” باز is an adverb “again” modifying the verb, and اژدهای دژم is the noun phrase “the grim dragon” (اژدهای = dragon, دژم = sullen/grim). Word order is the poetic VSO feel: the verb بغرید precedes the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s113-g42: همی is a continuative/iterative particle (here marking an ongoing or immediate action). آتش افروخت is a past tense verb phrase “kindled/struck up fire” (آتش = fire; افروخت = kindled). گفتی here functions as an act/utterance noun (the ‘saying’/word) — i.e. he uttered the word that follows — and بدم is that uttered exclamation; the clause strings the actions together (he roared, kindled fire, and then uttered/ cried the word).
segments.s113-g43: چراگاه is a locative noun “pasture/feeding place.” بگذاشت is a past tense verb here meaning “let go / set free / loosened” (classical use: let the steed go). Rخش (رخش) is the proper name of the steed, and آنزمان “then/at that moment” gives the time; the clause means that at the pasture Rakhsh was launched/let go at that time.
segments.s113-g44: نیارست is an archaic/poetic negative/inability form (here expressing that it was impossible to restrain / he could not refrain). It governs the following verbal infinitive رفتن “to go.” بر پهلوان uses the preposition بر to mark direction/target (“toward / against the champion”), so the full phrase means “could not refrain from going toward the champion.”
segments.s113-g45: دلش is the possessive construction “his heart” (دل + enclitic ش). زان is a causal/prepositional particle “from that / because of that.” شگفتی is the noun “amazement / wonder.” به دو نیم is a locative/result phrase “into two halves” (به = into), and بود is the past copula “was.” Together: “His heart, from that wonder, was into two halves.”
segments.s113-g46: کش here is an archaic/discourse particle introducing the next comment (roughly ‘for/indeed’ in poetic usage). از + noun marks source/cause: از رستم و اژدها = “from/of Rostam and the dragon.” بیم بود is the existential copular construction “there was fear” / “he had fear.” The clause therefore explains that he feared both Rostam and the dragon.
segments.s113-g47: هم is an additive particle “also / yet.” از بهر (classical compound) means “for the sake of / on behalf of / with respect to” (here “for Rostam”). دلش is “his heart,” and نارمید is a poetic adjective indicating lack of despair or being undaunted (the form is archaic/condensed). The clause says that, with respect to Rostam, his heart remained undismayed.
segments.s113-g48: چو is a comparative/simile particle here meaning “like / as.” باد دمان is a metaphorical phrase “the wind of the hem/skirt” (a classical image for a sudden gust / great speed). نزد رستم uses نزد to mark motion toward/near (to Rostam), and دوید is the past tense verb “ran.” Grammatically this is a simile: he ran to Rostam as if he were a gust of wind from the hem.

sentences.s113-s5: No one could pass there through the devs, the elephants, and the male lions.
sentences.s113-s6: He too came, but could not find release from the claw of the ill‑minded male dragon.
sentences.s113-s7: He turned his face toward Rakhsh the radiant; the steed began to gallop toward the village stream.
sentences.s113-s8: He kept striking the armored earth with his hoof; like thunder it roared and shook out its breath.
sentences.s113-s49: Before me — whether lion, demon, or elephant — lie the waterless desert and the sea of the Nile.
sentences.s113-s50: There are many ill‑wishers; but if there are only a few, when I rouse my anger they are but one before my eyes.
sentences.s113-s9: When Tahmatan awoke from sleep, his head became full — full of thought, full of battle.
sentences.s113-s10: He looked around the wilderness, and that grim dragon was gone.
sentences.s113-s11: Rakhsh (the steed) engaged the staring foe in combat; from that one he woke the sleeper.
sentences.s113-s12: Once again, when he fell asleep inside, that dragon came out of the darkness.
sentences.s113-s25: It roared and surged and tore up the soil; from his horseshoe the ground became all split and rent.
sentences.s113-s26: When Rostam awoke from pleasant sleep, he sprang up, glove in hand.
sentences.s113-s27: Thus the World‑Creator made it bright, so that the earth did not hide the dragon.
sentences.s113-s28: At that gloom Rostam saw him; he drew his light, sharp sword from the midst.
sentences.s113-s13: Rakhsh came up to Rostam's bedside and kept pawing the ground and scattering dust.
sentences.s113-s14: The sleeping man awoke again; he was startled and his visage turned pale.
sentences.s113-s15: He looked all over the plain from end to end, and saw nothing with his eyes except the darkness of night.
sentences.s113-s16: He said to that kind Rakhsh, 'Be awake, for the darkness of night will try to hide (things).'
sentences.s113-s33: My place is entirely on the plain; its lofty sky is my air.
sentences.s113-s34: No one can pass over its head on an eagle; no star sees its land asleep.
sentences.s113-s35: The dragon said to him, 'What is your name? For even the one who gave you life must lament for you.'
sentences.s113-s36: He answered thus: 'I am Rostam — from Dastān and from Sam and from my own might.'
sentences.s113-s1: From the plain there came a dragon, from which even an elephant could not find release.
sentences.s113-s2: That spot was his resting-place; no demon, for fear of him, went that way.
sentences.s113-s3: He came and saw the world-seeker sleeping; before him he saw a certain distressed horse.
sentences.s113-s4: He became full of thought to see what would appear, for his companion had rested in that place.
sentences.s113-s37: I will entrust my army — that single, vindictive one — to Rakhsh, the brave of the land.
sentences.s113-s38: He leapt at him into battle with the dragon; it did not come to an end, nor was it released from him.
sentences.s113-s39: When Rakhsh saw the dragon’s bodily strength, he sprang upon it in that fashion, together with Tajbakhsh.
sentences.s113-s40: He rubbed his ear; astonishment came upon him, and he seized the tall dragon with his teeth.
sentences.s113-s41: He tore its shoulder with his teeth like a lion; then the brave champion stood aghast.
sentences.s113-s42: He struck with the sword and hurled it off from his head; blood poured down from it like a river.
sentences.s113-s43: The ground vanished beneath his body; a spring of blood rose up from him.
sentences.s113-s44: When Rostam looked at that grim dragon, a sharp tail struck.
sentences.s113-s45: The whole plain lay beneath him completely; warm blood flowed from upon the dark earth.
sentences.s113-s46: Tahmatan remained astonished at him; he repeatedly invoked the name of God.
sentences.s113-s47: He went into the water and washed head and body; in the world no one strove against him except by the might of the world‑guardian.
sentences.s113-s48: To God he thus said: “O Just One, you gave me knowledge, strength, and glory.”
sentences.s113-s17: You keep me from sleep again; at my waking, haste seized you.
sentences.s113-s18: If this time you raise such an uprising, I'll cut off your head with a sharp sword.
sentences.s113-s19: I will go on foot toward Mazandaran; I will take a tiger, a sword, and a heavy mace.
sentences.s113-s20: Sīm‑rah fell into sleep; his head (then) from the tiger showed (or was) covered.
sentences.s113-s29: The spring clouds thundered and brought it; the earth became full of fire from the battle.
sentences.s113-s30: Thereupon the dragon said: “Speak the name; from now on you will not behold the world to your desire.”
sentences.s113-s31: It must not be that you, nameless, come into my hand; your soul shall depart from the dark body.
sentences.s113-s32: Thus spoke the cruel male dragon: ‘From my claw no one finds release.’
sentences.s113-s21: Again the grim dragon roared; he kindled fire and uttered the word “بدم.”
sentences.s113-s22: At the pasture Rakhsh was let loose; he could not refrain from going toward the champion.
sentences.s113-s23: His heart was split in two from that wonder; for he had fear of both Rostam and the dragon.
sentences.s113-s24: Yet, for Rostam his heart was not despairing; like a gust of the hem, he ran to Rostam.
